script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and today is Saturday, June 27, 2026 and The Portfolio Podcast QQQM update is as of June 27 2026 at 9:46 AM ET. The portfolio is sitting at ninety-eight thousand, three hundred and ninety-three dollars and fifty-two cents, down one point six one percent on the session — that is a loss of one thousand, six hundred and six dollars and forty-eight cents against our one hundred thousand dollar base. A rough Friday for technology names. Here is what drove it.

Apple is seeking White House clearance to buy memory chips from a blacklisted Chinese manufacturer. That headline hit early and it hit hard. The market is reading this two ways simultaneously — cost discipline or supply chain desperation — and right now, the pessimists are winning that argument. Stay with me.

Broad market context is thin on the macro side this morning, with no major economic prints crossing the wire ahead of the session close. What is clear is sector rotation. The narrative that has powered this portfolio for months — the AI infrastructure supercycle — is showing visible cracks this week, with capital visibly moving away from hyperscalers and semiconductor names toward energy, industrials, and rate-sensitive sectors. Microsoft was the lone bright spot among our top holdings, and we will get to why in a moment.

The top story today belongs to Apple, and it connects directly to that cold open. According to a Financial Times report cited by Bloomberg and published early this morning, Apple is actively pressing the White House for approval to purchase memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese semiconductor company that currently sits on the United States government's trade blacklist. The context matters here. The AI boom has fundamentally restructured the memory chip market. What was once one of the most oversupplied, commoditized corners of semiconductors has become a critical bottleneck for AI infrastructure buildout. Prices have surged. Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix have all reported record profitability on the back of that tightness. Apple, facing mounting cost pressure as it pushes AI features deeper into its hardware stack, is apparently looking at CXMT as a lower-cost alternative. The immediate market reaction was unambiguous — Apple shares sank. A separate Yahoo Finance headline this morning referenced price hikes hitting iPads and Macs, compounding the consumer sentiment concern. Meanwhile, a broader rotation story is developing, with analysts at multiple shops flagging that hyperscalers and AI-boom stocks may be passing the baton to other sectors. For QQQM holders, Apple is the single largest position at eight point nine seven percent of the portfolio. Today's move was consequential, and the CXMT story adds a geopolitical overhang that does not resolve quickly.

Apple closed at two hundred and eighty-three dollars and seventy-eight cents, down nine dollars and thirty cents, a loss of three point one seven percent on the session. That is the largest single-stock drag on the portfolio today by dollar weight. Beyond the CXMT headline, the broader rotation away from AI-adjacent names created a headwind Apple could not overcome. The geopolitical dimension here is real — White House approval processes for blacklisted entity purchases are not fast, and the story keeps the stock in an uncomfortable news cycle. Watch for any official response from the administration over the weekend.

Turning to Microsoft — the one genuine bright spot in today's tape. Microsoft closed at three hundred and seventy-two dollars and ninety-seven cents, up seven dollars and fifty-one cents, a gain of two point zero five percent. At eight point four two percent of the portfolio, that move provided meaningful cushion against the broader selloff. The catalyst appears tied to continued confidence in Microsoft's AI infrastructure positioning. A Bernstein note circulating this morning highlighted that Google Cloud emerged as the leading AI cloud platform in the first quarter of twenty twenty-six, but Microsoft's involvement in high-profile projects — including the Chevron-Microsoft AI data center power initiative cited in connection with Caterpillar's Russell Top 50 addition — kept buy-side interest intact. A separate piece comparing Microsoft and Alphabet revenue trends noted that while Alphabet has outpaced Microsoft in top-line revenue for recent quarters, both are delivering steady long-term growth. Microsoft is holding its ground, and today it showed it.

NVIDIA closed at one hundred and ninety-two dollars and fifty-three cents, down six dollars and forty-seven cents, a decline of three point two five percent. At eight point one five percent of the portfolio, that is a significant weight dragging performance. News flow around NVIDIA today was noisy but not directly company-specific. One piece explored the case for data center infrastructure funds as an alternative to chip-heavy ETFs like SMH. Another flagged SpaceX borrowing twenty billion dollars to fund AI ambitions — a downstream demand signal for compute, but not an immediate NVIDIA catalyst. Sentiment indicators are also worth noting: one headline referenced a market fear gauge hitting extreme fear territory, citing Warren Buffett's historical commentary on volatility. NVIDIA remains a core AI infrastructure name, but the rotation dynamic flagged this week is a real short-term headwind.

Over at Amazon, shares closed at two hundred and thirty-two dollars and sixty-nine cents, down one dollar and fifty-eight cents, off zero point six seven percent. At five point five two percent of the portfolio, the damage was contained. The relevant news today centered on Rubrik's expanded partnership with Cognizant and an integration with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, positioning Rubrik as a governance layer for autonomous AI agents running on AWS. That is an incremental positive for Amazon's cloud ecosystem story, reinforcing AWS as a platform of choice for enterprise AI workloads. The move in the stock was modest, and the fundamental AI cloud narrative remains intact.

Broadcom was the hardest hit name on a percentage basis today. Shares closed at three hundred and sixty-five dollars and two cents, down seventeen dollars and five cents — a drop of four point four six percent. At four point six one percent of the portfolio, that hurts. There was no company-specific news crossing the wire today, but Broadcom trades in tight sympathy with the broader semiconductor complex, and with NVIDIA and the AI chip trade under pressure, Broadcom absorbed collateral damage. The stock has been a strong performer on custom ASIC demand tied to hyperscaler AI spending — that thesis is intact, but the rotation narrative is creating near-term turbulence.

Meanwhile, Meta Platforms closed at five hundred and fifty dollars and twenty-five cents, down seven dollars and forty-two cents, off one point three three percent. At four point four eight percent of the portfolio, it was a quiet but negative session. No Meta-specific headlines crossed today. The communication services sector broadly tracked lower alongside the AI rotation trade. Meta's AI infrastructure spending story remains a longer-term catalyst, but the stock offered no shelter today.

Alphabet Class A, ticker GOOGL, closed at three hundred and thirty-seven dollars and thirty-nine cents, down seven dollars and ninety cents, a decline of two point two nine percent. The Bernstein note this morning was actually constructive on Google Cloud, calling it the leading AI cloud platform after a strong first quarter. The stock's decline today appears macro and rotation-driven rather than fundamental. Alphabet Class C, ticker GOOG, closed at three hundred and thirty-four dollars and sixty-nine cents, down ten dollars and thirty-five cents, off three point zero zero percent. No news specific to GOOG today — the move largely mirrors Class A with a slight discount, consistent with the share structure differential.

Tesla closed at three hundred and seventy-nine dollars and seventy-one cents, up four dollars and eighteen cents, a gain of one point one one percent. At three point five four percent of the portfolio, it was a quiet but welcome green number. No Tesla-specific news today, but the stock bucked the broader tech selloff, likely benefiting from the rotation into non-AI growth names that characterized today's session.

Finally, Costco closed at nine hundred and fifty-two dollars and fifty-four cents, down eight dollars and fifty-five cents, off zero point eight nine percent. At two point eight one percent of the portfolio, it was the smallest weight and a modest drag. No Costco-specific news today. The consumer staples name typically provides relative stability, and today's decline was well within normal range.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any White House response to Apple's reported request for approval to purchase CXMT chips — that story will move the stock on Monday if there is official commentary. Second, watch for any weekend developments on AI infrastructure spending announcements from hyperscalers, particularly given the rotation narrative now building momentum. Third, monitor semiconductor sector sentiment broadly heading into the new week — NVIDIA and Broadcom are both sensitive to any shift in AI capex guidance language.

That is your Saturday QQQM update. The next episode drops at five PM ET on the next trading day. Overnight, keep one eye on any geopolitical response to the Apple-CXMT story — that is the single thread most likely to move this portfolio when markets reopen. I am Josh Weinberg. Stay disciplined.
